['Fix system Eigen 5.0.x detection in CMake

Eigen 5.0's installed Eigen3Config.cmake only defines the Eigen3::Eigen
target; it no longer sets the legacy EIGEN3_FOUND / EIGEN3_INCLUDE_DIR
variables that Eigen 3.4 provided. find_package(Eigen3) sets the
case-sensitive Eigen3_FOUND, so the old if(EIGEN3_FOUND) check never
fired with system Eigen 5.0.x: builds with USE_SYSTEM_EIGEN_INSTALL=ON
silently fell through to the bundled-Eigen fallback. Switch the check
to Eigen3_FOUND and read the include directory from the target's
INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES.

The bundled-Eigen fallback in the same block invoked
tools/optional_modules.py, which does not exist. The script is
tools/optional_submodules.py (checkout_eigen was added there in
#155955, but the cmake reference was never updated). Because
execute_process did not check its result, the failure was silent and
the fallback never actually checked out the pinned Eigen. Corrected
the path so the fallback works.

Revives #172980 by @heylinn, approved by @malfet and closed only
because of an unsigned CLA; credit for the detection fix goes to her.

Test Plan:

Installed Eigen 5.0.1 into a local prefix (WSL Ubuntu, gcc 15,
CMake 3.31):

```
curl -sSLO https://gitlab.com/libeigen/eigen/-/archive/5.0.1/eigen-5.0.1.tar.gz
tar xzf eigen-5.0.1.tar.gz
cmake -S eigen-5.0.1 -B eigen-build -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=$HOME/eigen-install -DEIGEN_BUILD_TESTING=OFF -DEIGEN_BUILD_DOC=OFF
cmake --install eigen-build
```

Configure with system Eigen (previously fell back to bundled):

```
cmake -S pytorch -B build-system -G Ninja -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=$HOME/eigen-install -DUSE_SYSTEM_EIGEN_INSTALL=ON -DBLAS=Eigen -DBUILD_PYTHON=OFF -DBUILD_TEST=OFF -DUSE_CUDA=OFF -DUSE_DISTRIBUTED=OFF -DUSE_MKLDNN=OFF -DUSE_FBGEMM=OFF -DUSE_XNNPACK=OFF -DUSE_PYTORCH_QNNPACK=OFF -DUSE_KINETO=OFF -DUSE_NNPACK=OFF -DUSE_NUMPY=OFF -DUSE_OPENMP=OFF
```

now prints "-- Found system Eigen 5.0.1 at
$HOME/eigen-install/include/eigen3" and completes ("Configuring done",
"Generating done"). The default configure (same flags minus
CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH and USE_SYSTEM_EIGEN_INSTALL) still prints "-- Using
third party subdirectory Eigen." and completes, so the bundled path is
unchanged. Also verified the fallback script reference directly:
`python3 tools/optional_modules.py checkout_eigen` fails with "No such
file or directory" while `python3 tools/optional_submodules.py
checkout_eigen` runs and no-ops when third_party/eigen is present.

Fixes #172949']

["Fix MKL VML first-call dispatch race producing silent low-precision results

ATen routes contiguous float unary ops (sqrt, asin, exp, log, sin, ...) to
MKL VML, calling e.g. vmsSqrt(n, in, out, VML_HA | ...) from each intra-op
worker thread on its chunk of the tensor. oneMKL resolves each VML
function's (ISA, accuracy) kernel dispatch lazily on that function's first
call. When that first call happens concurrently on several worker threads
(a fresh process's first sizable op, e.g. a dataloader worker's first
batch, especially under CPU oversubscription), one thread can execute a
mismatched kernel variant for that single call. Captured bit-exactly on
oneMKL 2024.2 (Build 20240605) on a Xeon Platinum 8481C: the AVX2 EP
(low-accuracy) kernel is served for one thread's chunk despite VML_HA
being requested per call and AVX512 being the resolved ISA. The affected
chunk of torch.sqrt comes out up to ~4000 ulp off; the next call
dispatches correctly, so identical reruns are clean and the corruption
looks like a transient heisenbug. Downstream, sqrt errors of this size
can turn asin(y/r) with |y/r| near 1 into NaN.

Fix: resolve the dispatch once per function and precision with a
serialized 1-element warm-up call under c10::call_once before any
concurrent use. Steady-state cost is one atomic load per call, and
steady-state outputs are bitwise unchanged.

Alternatives considered: the race itself is inside closed-source oneMKL
(reported separately), and serializing every VML call would cost
throughput. The once-per-function warm-up closes the window regardless of
when oneMKL fixes the underlying race.

Test plan:

lintrunner reports no issues on the change. Built CPU-only with
statically linked oneMKL 2024.2 (which reproduces the race at a much
higher rate than the official wheels) and ran a storm repro: waves of 104
spawned processes, each bitwise-comparing its first-ever
torch.sqrt(393216 floats) against an immediate rerun in the same process.
Repro script attached to the PR.

```
# unpatched build
python mkl_vml_race_repro.py 40 104  # 216 mismatched first-calls / 4160
# patched build
python mkl_vml_race_repro.py 50 104  # 0 mismatched first-calls / 5200
```

Steady-state numerics are bitwise identical between the patched and
unpatched builds, and the VML-routed ops (sqrt, asin, exp, log, sin, tan,
tanh, acos, atan, erf) sanity-check finite through the warm-up path.

Authored with the help of an AI assistant.", 'Note affected oneMKL versions and issue link in vml.h comment

The dispatch race reproduces on oneMKL 2024.2.0-2024.2.2 (the pinned CI /
wheel version) and stops reproducing at oneMKL 2025.0, so record the
affected range and when the workaround can be retired.

Test plan: storm repro (see issue) against unpatched builds linked to
oneMKL 2024.2.0/.1/.2 (216/295/224 corrupted first-calls out of 4160
each) and 2025.0 / 2025.2 / 2026.1 (0 out of 4160 each); patched builds
show 0 across all versions tested.

Authored with the help of an AI assistant.']

Followup - Full git fetch on tag pushes so release manywheel builds detect the tag (#187047)

## Problem

The unified inline manywheel build jobs (`cpu` / `cpu-aarch64` / `cuda` / `cuda-aarch64`) check out a pinned commit SHA with a shallow `fetch-depth: 2`. On a release tag push (e.g. `v2.13.0-rc1`) the tag ref is never written into that shallow checkout, so `tagged_version()` in `.ci/pytorch/binary_populate_env.sh` (`git describe --tags --exact`) fails. The build then falls through to the `<version>.dev<DATE>` default, producing wheels versioned `2.13.0.devYYYYMMDD` and pinning triton to a `+git<hash>` version that does not exist on the test index.

## Root cause

#187001 added `fetch-tags: true` but kept `fetch-depth: 2`. `fetch-tags` is ineffective when `ref` is a bare SHA and the fetch is shallow -- git does not write the tag ref locally. The proven path (the `checkout-pytorch` action) works because it uses `fetch-depth: 0`, where a full fetch brings all tags regardless.

## Fix

Do a full fetch (`fetch-depth: 0`) on tag pushes and keep the shallow `fetch-depth: 2` for nightlies:

```yaml
fetch-depth: ${{ github.ref_type != 'tag' && 2 || 0 }}
fetch-tags: ${{ github.ref_type == 'tag' }}
```

### Why the expression is written this way

GitHub Actions has no ternary, so the `A && B || C` idiom is used -- but it only works when `B` is **truthy**. The naive `github.ref_type == 'tag' && 0 || 2` is broken because `0` is falsy: on a real tag push it evaluates `true && 0` -> `0`, then `0 || 2` -> `2`, so it **always** yields `2` and the full-fetch branch is unreachable. An earlier revision shipped this broken form, which is why tag builds kept getting `fetch-depth: 2`.

Negating the condition keeps the truthy literal (`2`) as the `&&` operand so the value is never swallowed:

| trigger | expression | result |
|---|---|---|
| tag | `false && 2` -> false -> `false \|\| 0` | **0** (full fetch) |
| branch / nightly | `true && 2` -> 2 | **2** (shallow) |

With a full fetch the tag is present, `git describe --tags --exact` succeeds, and the build resolves to the release version with a clean triton requirement.

> Needs to be cherry-picked onto `release/2.13` (superseding the broken expression) for the RC builds to pick it up.

## Test Plan

Regenerated the workflows from the template and confirmed only the two linux manywheel workflows change, each of the 4 unified build jobs getting the conditional `fetch-depth` and `fetch-tags`:

```

git diff --stat .github/workflows
grep -c "github.ref_type != 'tag' && 2 || 0" \
  .github/workflows/generated-linux-binary-manywheel-nightly.yml \
  .github/workflows/generated-linux-aarch64-binary-manywheel-nightly.yml
```

Validated the regenerated YAML parses and passes actionlint (the only reported shellcheck warnings are pre-existing `SC1090` on `source "${BINARY_ENV_FILE}"`):

```

  .github/workflows/generated-linux-aarch64-binary-manywheel-nightly.yml

```

Only the `wheel-build` checkout is changed; the libtorch `*-extract` job uses a sparse checkout of the already-built wheel artifact and does not need the tag.

> Needs to be cherry-picked onto `release/2.13` for the RC builds to pick it up.

Co-authored-by: Yu, Guangye <guangye.yu@intel.com>", '[SDPA] Fix 32-bit offset overflow in mem-efficient attention forward with attn_bias (#187684)

[SDPA] Fix 32-bit offset overflow in mem-efficient attention forward with attn_bias (#187225)

## Summary

`F.scaled_dot_product_attention` with the **EFFICIENT_ATTENTION** backend and an
explicit `attn_mask` **silently returns garbage** for every query row past
`2^32 / seq_len` once `num_queries * num_keys > 2^32` (i.e. S > 65536 for square
self-attention). No error, no warning, no NaN — the corrupted values look
plausible, which makes this extremely hard to attribute downstream (we found it
as silently diverging RL training metrics).

## Root cause

In `kernel_forward.h`, `query_start` is `uint32_t`:

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/545c55c1724bafed6772a0b85fecfd09f9aa0dbb/aten/src/ATen/native/transformers/cuda/mem_eff_attention/kernel_forward.h#L635

and the per-row offset into the bias tensor is computed as
`query_start * p.bias_strideM` where `bias_strideM` is `int32_t`, so the multiply
happens in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic and wraps modulo 2^32:

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/545c55c1724bafed6772a0b85fecfd09f9aa0dbb/aten/src/ATen/native/transformers/cuda/mem_eff_attention/kernel_forward.h#L811

Past `row = 2^32 / seq_len` the kernel reads the mask from wrapped addresses
(typically an earlier row of the same tensor), producing wrong attention for
those rows. Corruption onset at exactly `2^32 / S` (not `2^31 / S`) matches the
unsigned arithmetic.

The dropout RNG `skipahead` offset in the same kernel has the same 32-bit
multiply (`(query_start + thread_i) * p.num_keys_absolute`). The backward kernel
computes the equivalent offset with an `int64_t` `query_start`
(`kernel_backward.h`), so with dropout + large seq_len the forward and backward
dropout masks diverge past the same boundary, corrupting gradients.

The batch/head-level strides (`bias_strideB`/`bias_strideH`) were already
widened to `int64_t` previously; the per-row offset was missed. Note these
kernels were vendored from xformers, which has since removed its in-tree CUTLASS
FMHA sources, so the fix needs to land here.

## Fix

Compute both offsets in 64-bit, matching `kernel_backward.h`. Two one-line
casts; in-bounds behavior is bit-identical.

## Reproduction (any sm80+ GPU, bf16)

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.nn.attention import SDPBackend, sdpa_kernel

S, HD = 73728, 64                        # S^2 = 1.27 * 2^32
torch.manual_seed(0)
q = torch.randn(1, 1, S, HD, device="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16) * 0.1
k = torch.randn(1, 1, S, HD, device="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16) * 0.1
v = torch.randn(1, 1, S, HD, device="cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16)
idx = torch.arange(S, device="cuda")
causal = (idx[:, None] >= idx[None, :])[None, None]

ref = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)   # ground truth
with sdpa_kernel(SDPBackend.EFFICIENT_ATTENTION):
    out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, attn_mask=causal)

B = 2**32 // S   # predicted corruption onset = row 58254
print((out - ref)[0, 0, :B].abs().max())   # ~6e-5  (bf16 noise)
print((out - ref)[0, 0, B:].abs().max())   # ~0.39  (garbage)
```

Observed on `2.14.0.dev20260612+cu130` (H200, sm90) — corruption onset is
exactly `2^32 // S` for every S tested (73728→58254, 81920→52428, 98304→43690,
90000→47721). The MATH backend with the same mask tensor is correct everywhere,
confirming the mask contents are fine and the bug is in the kernel's indexing.
`is_causal=True` (no mask tensor) is unaffected.

## Test plan

- Added `test_mem_eff_attention_large_seq_len_attn_mask_index_overflow`
  (gated by `@largeTensorTest("12GB", "cuda")`): explicit additive causal mask
  vs `is_causal=True` on the same backend at seq_len=70000. Fails before this
  fix (max abs diff 0.54 past row 61356, exact 0 before it), passes after.
- Verified the kernel change directly on H200: compiled `kernel_forward.h`
  (pristine vs patched, same CUTLASS pin) into a standalone extension driving
  `AttentionKernel<bf16, Sm80, true, 64, 64, 64>` with identical S=73728
  inputs. Pristine reproduces the corruption bit-for-bit with the stock binary
  (max diff 0.39 past row 58254); patched returns bf16-noise-level diff
  (6.1e-5) everywhere, and is bit-identical to pristine for all in-bounds rows.

change and verification were reviewed end-to-end by the submitter.

Approved by: https://github.com/zou3519', 'Fix Windows libtorch x86_64 and arm64 packages overwriting each other (#187973)

Fix Windows libtorch x86_64 and arm64 packages overwriting each other (#187837)

Fixes #187812

## Problem

The Windows libtorch CD packages for x86_64 and arm64 are generated with the **same filename** and uploaded to the **same S3 location**, so the arm64 build clobbers the x86_64 one. Users downloading the Windows x64 libtorch release actually receive Aarch64 binaries (`c10.dll: PE32+ executable (DLL) (GUI) Aarch64`).

## Root cause

`compute_zip_prefix()` in `.ci/libtorch/extract_libtorch_from_wheel.py` returned `libtorch-win-shared-with-deps` for every Windows build regardless of architecture (the docstring even named `libtorch-win-arm64-shared-with-deps`, but the code never produced it). Both x86_64 and arm64 are CPU builds, so the upload subfolder (`DESIRED_CUDA=cpu`) and the zip filename were identical, and the S3 object key is the zip filename.

The arch-blind naming was introduced in #174753 (which added the wheel-extraction approach); the collision became live once the windows-arm64 wheel workflow started running the same extraction in #181586.

## Fix

Thread architecture through the extraction so arm64 produces `libtorch-win-arm64-shared-with-deps-{version}.zip` while x86_64 keeps its existing `libtorch-win-shared-with-deps-{version}.zip` name (preserving the public download URL). `generate_libtorch_extraction_configs` also gives the arm64 job a distinct `build_name` so the GitHub artifact and job names no longer alias.

## Test Plan

Verified prefix logic:

```
x86_64 -> libtorch-win-shared-with-deps
arm64  -> libtorch-win-arm64-shared-with-deps
```

Regenerated the binary build workflows (`python .github/scripts/generate_ci_workflows.py`); the windows-arm64 wheel workflow now passes `--arch arm64` and uploads a distinct `libtorch-arm64-cpu-shared-with-deps-release` artifact, while the x86_64 workflow is unchanged apart from an explicit `--arch x86_64`.

Co-authored-by: atalman <atalman@meta.com>', "Fix bmm outer product Triton launch on non-current CUDA device (#187983)

Fix bmm outer product Triton launch on non-current CUDA device (#187733)

The Python native bmm outer-product override launches a Triton kernel for
CUDA inputs shaped like `(B, M, 1) x (B, 1, N)`. Unlike the generated C++
ATen CUDA path, this Python dispatch path does not get an automatic CUDA
device guard before launching the kernel. In vLLM multimodal tests, the
model hooks can run the relevant rotary embedding buffers and inputs on
`cuda:1` while the process current device is still `cuda:0`; Triton then
launches on the wrong device/stream and rejects the `cuda:1` pointers with
`ValueError: Pointer argument cannot be accessed from Triton (cpu tensor?)`.

Guard the Triton call when the input device is not already current, while
leaving the common already-current path direct to avoid unnecessary Python
context manager overhead. Also require both bmm inputs to be on the same CUDA
device before selecting the override, so mismatched CUDA inputs fall back to
native bmm and report the normal same-device error instead of reaching the
Triton kernel.

I considered guarding unconditionally, but a small same-device benchmark showed
that the Python context manager adds measurable overhead for small outer-product
bmm calls. The conditional guard keeps the fix scoped to the non-current-device
case that needs it.

Approved by: https://github.com/Skylion007

* [MPS] Migrate argmin/argmax from MPSGraph to Metal kernels (#187304)

Replaces the MPSGraph-based argmax/argmin implementation with three native Metal kernels mirroring the value_reduction layout introduced for amax/amin/all/any in #180752: a generic NormParams kernel for arbitrary reduction patterns, an inner-dim kernel (one simdgroup per row, simd_arg_reduce collapse) for contiguous reductions over the last dim, and an outer-dim kernel (TG_X x TG_Y tile, shared-mem pair tree reduce) for contiguous reductions over dim 0. The host-side dispatcher in ReduceOps.mm picks inner/outer when the input and output are contiguous and exactly one of those dims is reduced, and falls back to the generic kernel otherwise; full reduction (dim=None) is handled via a contiguous flatten so the returned linear index matches the standard as-if-contiguous convention.

The MPSGraph path used to cast bfloat16 to float32 before the reduction, so bf16 is where the migration wins the most (e.g. 4096x4096 dim=0 argmax goes 1518us -> 294us = 5.17x on M4 Max); fp16/fp32 dim=0 hit 2.4-3.5x and dim=1 hit 1.1-1.3x.

Correctness: the natural simd_argmax(val, idx) helper ties on lowest LANE, but when a single lane scans multiple positions its stored idx is not necessarily the lowest one carrying the winning value, so the kernels do a two-step simd_max + simd_min-on-eff_idx pattern instead (NaN lanes count as winners so first-NaN-in-source-order wins). The shared-memory tree reduction in the outer kernel uses the same strict-better-or-equal-with-lower-idx predicate. The NaN-propagating "should-replace" predicates argmax_replace / argmin_replace are factored into c10/metal/reduction_utils.h so they sit next to simd_argmax/simd_argmin, which already encode the same NaN-as-winner logic via simd_ballot.

TODO: argmin/argmax and amin/amax now share the (general, inner, outer) kernel shape, the per-thread-scan + simd-collapse skeleton, and the address-math helpers. Unifying them under a single Reducer concept (per-thread accumulator + finalize hook, with NCHAINS as a property of the reducer) would let the three kernel grids be written once and instantiated for both ValueReducer<Op, Load, NCHAINS=8> and ArgReducer<Op, NCHAINS=1>. Worth doing in a follow-up.

Benchmark (median of 500 iters on M4 Max, agent_space/bench_argminmax_pr.py):

| op     | shape     | dtype    | mode | baseline (us) | new (us) | speedup |
|--------|-----------|----------|------|--------------:|---------:|--------:|
| argmax | 4096x4096 | bfloat16 | dim0 |          1518 |      294 |  5.17x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | bfloat16 | dim1 |           991 |      315 |  3.15x  |
| argmax | 4096x4096 | float16  | dim0 |          1024 |      290 |  3.53x  |

Fix build_with_debinfo.py broken by CONFIGURE_DEPENDS globbing (#186780)

## Summary

`tools/build_with_debinfo.py` recompiles named source files with `-g` so you
can get debug info for a targeted file without a full (slow, ABI-incompatible)
debug build. It is referenced in `CONTRIBUTING.md`, and it broke on current
`main`: running it just prints `Nothing to do` and rebuilds nothing.

## Root cause

The tool derived its compile/link commands from `ninja -j1 -v -n torch_python`
(a dry run) and rewrote `-O2`/`-O3` to `-g`. The build now uses
`file(GLOB ... CONFIGURE_DEPENDS)`, which wires a glob-verification step into
`build.ninja`'s own regeneration (`CMakeFiles/VerifyGlobs.cmake` +
`regenerate-during-build`). In dry-run (`-n`) mode ninja cannot run that check
or reload the regenerated graph, so `ninja -n <target>` only ever reports the
regeneration step and never the real compile/link commands. The tool parsed an
empty plan and no-op'd.

## Fix

Source the commands from places the glob-check does not gate:

- the per-file compile command from `build/compile_commands.json` (rewrite
  `-O2`/`-O3` -> `-g`);
- the `libtorch_python` link command from `ninja -t commands torch_python`
  (a graph walk, not a dry run; strip ninja's `: && ... && :` wrapper).

Then symlink `build/lib` into `torch/lib` as before. The tool now rebuilds
exactly the files you name plus the relink, rather than touching sources and
relying on ninja's staleness detection. The old `>100 stale items` guard was a
dry-run artifact and is removed; the tool now requires `compile_commands.json`
and an already-built tree.

## Tests

- **Unit tests** (`tools/test/test_build_with_debinfo.py`) for the pure
  plan-derivation helpers: the `-O` -> `-g` rewrite, `compile_commands.json`
  indexing, the command/arguments entry forms, and the `ninja -t commands`
  link extraction.
- **Build-tree smoke check**: a new `--dry-run` mode plus a step in
  `.ci/pytorch/build.sh` that, after the Linux build, asserts the emitted plan
  contains a `-g` compile and the `libtorch_python` relink. This exercises the
  real `compile_commands.json` and `ninja -t commands` and fails loudly if a
  future build-system change breaks those data sources -- the class of bug
  here, which unit tests alone would not catch. `--dry-run` only reads the
  tree, so it does not dirty the checkout.

## Test plan

Validated on a real editable CUDA build of `viable/strict`:

```
python tools/build_with_debinfo.py --verbose torch/csrc/Module.cpp
```
recompiles `Module.cpp.o` with `.debug_info`, relinks `libtorch_python.so`
(which then carries `.debug_info`), symlinks it into `torch/lib`, and
`import torch` still works.

```
python tools/build_with_debinfo.py --dry-run torch/csrc/Module.cpp
```
exits 0, prints the plan, and leaves the git tree clean; the build.sh `-g` and
`libtorch_python` plan assertions both pass against that output.

```
python -m pytest tools/test/test_build_with_debinfo.py
```
-> 9 passed.

['[inductor] Accept `implicit` kwarg in `expand` lowering (#188758)

Summary:

The Inductor lowering for `aten.expand.default` at `torch/_inductor/lowering.py` was declared as `def expand(x, sizes):`, missing the `implicit` kwarg from the aten schema `aten::expand(Tensor(a) self, SymInt[] size, *, bool implicit=False)`.

Autograd emits `aten.expand.default(..., implicit=False)` internally — for example, from `mean_backward` and broadcast-backward paths. When dynamo captures such a graph and hands it to Inductor, the `register_lowering` wrapper forwards kwargs unchanged to the lowering, and `expand()` raises `TypeError: expand() got an unexpected keyword argument 'implicit'`. Every backward compile that flows through an autograd-generated expand fails with this error.

`implicit` is autograd-internal metadata (per aten's docs, "an internal flag used by PyTorch's autograd machinery") that does not affect the produced tensor. This diff makes the lowering accept and discard the kwarg. No output semantics change.

Test Plan:
Added a regression test `test_expand_implicit_kwarg` in `caffe2/test/inductor/test_torchinductor.py` that calls `torch.ops.aten.expand.default(x, sizes, implicit=False)` inside a compiled fn — mirrors what autograd emits.

```
$ buck2 test mode/opt //caffe2/test/inductor:test_inductor -- test_expand_implicit_kwarg
Tests finished: Pass 1. Fail 0.
```

Also verified end-to-end with an out-of-tree MTIA backward-compile harness — a suite of 7 minimal backward tests (`mean(a).backward()`, `mean(a * b).backward()`, `mean(a * b.unsqueeze(-1)).backward()`, `mean(a * b.expand(...)).backward()`, etc.) all failed pre-fix with the `implicit` `TypeError` and all pass post-fix.

Authored with AI assistant (Claude Code).

Differential Revision: D110394927', '[inductor] Rewind block_ptr across outer loop in nested reductions (#188771)

Summary:

For a fused broadcast + reduction (e.g. `mean(a * b)` where `a:[N]` and `b:[N, 1]` broadcast to `[N, N]`), Inductor's Triton codegen emits nested `for` loops over the two reduction axes. Block pointers are threaded through the loops and advanced with `tl.advance` at the suffix of each level.

The bookkeeping in `TritonKernel` recorded a pointer's per-level advancement only when that advancement was non-zero (`triton.py` around L3999-4005 skipped identity offsets). For a pointer that is constant along the outer axis but stepped along the inner axis, this meant no entry was recorded for the outer axis, so no `tl.advance` was emitted at the outer-loop suffix. On backends that lower the Python `for` to `scf.for` with the block pointer as an iter-arg (e.g. Triton-MTIA), the SSA value yielded at the end of the outer iteration was the pointer already stepped through all inner iterations. Subsequent outer iterations would then load out-of-bounds, silently returning padding (zero) and dropping most of the reduction. For a `6144x6144` case with `R0_BLOCK=R1_BLOCK=32` the observed result was `1/192` of the true value.

**The bug is generic, not MTIA-specific.** Triton lowers every backend's Python for-loop to `scf.for`, and any variable reassigned inside the loop (like `block_ptr = tl.advance(block_ptr, ...)`) becomes an iter-arg carried across iterations. CUDA Triton is mechanically susceptible to the same OOB-load pattern. That the bug hasn't surfaced on CUDA before is likely a combination of (1) MTIA's autotuner floors `R0_BLOCK` at 32 whereas CUDA typically picks a much larger tile, so on CUDA the "iteration 0 only" truncation drops a much smaller fraction of the reduction and is easy to attribute to BF16 accumulation noise; (2) Inductor's scheduler more often avoids nested-reduction codepaths on CUDA for broadcast-heavy shapes; and (3) most CUDA tests use zero-mean inputs where a truncated outer-product reduction is close to zero anyway. Landing this as an upstream Inductor fix rather than an MTIA-only workaround.

The fix records every pointer at every reduction level, including zero offsets, so the existing outer-suffix subtraction `outer_step - inner_step * inner_num_iter` can compute the correct rewind (e.g. `0 - 32*192 = -6144`). To keep the emitted kernel body clean, the emit site now drops `tl.advance(ptr, [0, ...])` lines whose final advancement collapses to zero. Backends that keep the block pointer as a Python-local rebound each iteration are unaffected; backends that thread it through SSA iter-args now see the rewind they need.

Test Plan:
Added a regression test `test_nested_reduction_broadcast_outer_product` in `caffe2/test/inductor/test_torchinductor.py` that exercises `(a * b).sum()` and `torch.mean(a * b)` on `[N]` and `[N, 1]` inputs at `N=1024`, using non-zero-mean inputs so a truncated reduction cannot hide behind BF16 noise.

```
$ buck2 test mode/opt //caffe2/test/inductor:test_inductor -- test_nested_reduction_broadcast_outer_product
```

Also verified out-of-tree on MTIA-Triton with a shifted-mean bcast harness (`randn + 3.0` at `N=6144`):

| Case | Pre-fix `mtia_err / cpu_bf16_err` | Post-fix ratio |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `sum([N] * [N,1])` | 689x wrong | 1.0 (matches) |
| `mean([N] * [N,1], shift=10)` | 436x wrong | 1.0 (matches) |
| `mean([N,22] * [N,1])` (production LAMP shape) | 1.0 (unaffected) | 1.0 (unaffected) |

Confirmed the generated Triton IR after the fix contains the missing outer-loop rewind: `tt.advance %8#1, [-6144]`.

Authored with AI assistant (Claude Code).

["[Decomp] Fix multi_margin_loss to use 1D indexing for weight

Fixes issue where weight[target] used 2D advanced indexing which caused
problems with inductor lowering on XPU backend.

The decomposition was doing:
  target = target.unsqueeze(1)  # [batch] -> [batch, 1]
  ...
  z = z * weight[target]  # 2D indexing: weight[[batch, 1]]

This 2D advanced indexing doesn't lower correctly in inductor for XPU.

Fix: Keep original 1D target and use explicit unsqueeze:
  target_1d = target  # [batch]
  target = target.unsqueeze(1)  # [batch, 1] for gather
  ...
  z = z * weight[target_1d].unsqueeze(1)  # 1D indexing + explicit unsqueeze

This matches how native CUDA/XPU kernels work (scalar indexing in loop)
and is easier for inductor to optimize and lower correctly.

Fixes pytorch/pytorch#178992

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>"]

['Preserve pipeline buffers during dynamic metadata inference

PipelineStage dynamic metadata inference runs representative forwards and backwards before the first runtime microbatch. That is needed to infer runtime metadata, but the eager executions can mutate module buffers through forward code or backward hooks. Those mutations leak into the real training step even though metadata inference is not part of training.

Save and restore the stage module buffers around dynamic forward and backward metadata inference so those eager probes can observe the same buffer values without publishing their mutations. The restore is scoped to dynamic inference only; static metadata still avoids the eager probes entirely.

Add a regression test with a module that mutates a buffer in both forward and a backward hook, then verifies PipelineStage preparation leaves the buffer unchanged.

['Fix multinomial without replacement silently sampling zero-probability categories

torch.multinomial(input, n_sample, replacement=False) could return categories
whose probability is zero. The without-replacement path samples via Gumbel
top-k: it computes q = input / Exponential(1) and takes topk(q, n_sample). A
zero-weight category gets key q = 0 while every nonzero category gets a strictly
positive key, so as soon as n_sample exceeds the number of categories with
nonzero probability, topk is forced to return zero-probability indices. The only
guard on this path checked n_sample <= input.size(-1) (the total number of
categories) rather than the number of nonzero categories, so e.g.
torch.multinomial(torch.tensor([0., 10., 3., 0.]), 4, replacement=False)
returned indices 0 and 3 despite their zero weight. The documented behavior is
to raise in this case.

Fix: in multinomial_out, when sampling without replacement, assert that every
distribution has at least n_sample categories with nonzero probability. This
reuses the at::_assert_async mechanism already used for the adjacent
zero-probability check (#134818), so it introduces no host synchronization, and
a single host-level check covers CPU, CUDA and MPS because all
without-replacement sampling flows through this fast path.

The stronger guard exposed a latent issue in the multinomial OpInfo sample
generator, which built weights with make_tensor(low=0). Its replacement=False
cases draw n_sample equal to the category count, and in bfloat16 small weights
round to exactly 0.0, so the (now correctly rejected) input had fewer nonzero
categories than requested. This made test_schema_check's
test_schema_correctness_multinomial_cpu_bfloat16 fail. Fixed by generating the
sample weights with low=1 so no weight rounds to zero in any dtype; the input is
still a valid multinomial weight tensor.

Fixes #125388

Test Plan:
Built CPU-only from source and ran the multinomial tests:

    python -m pytest test/test_torch.py -k "multinomial and cpu" -q
    # 11 passed, 6 skipped

    python -m pytest test/test_schema_check.py -k "multinomial" -q

The added regression test test_multinomial_without_replacement_zero_prob fails
without the fix (RuntimeError not raised) and passes with it. The OpInfo sample
change was verified to produce zero without-replacement precondition violations
across float32/float64/float16/bfloat16 over thousands of seeds, where the
previous low=0 generator violated it for bfloat16.

This PR was authored with the assistance of an AI coding assistant.']

['[inductor] Accept `implicit` kwarg in `expand` lowering (#188758)

Summary:

The Inductor lowering for `aten.expand.default` at `torch/_inductor/lowering.py` was declared as `def expand(x, sizes):`, missing the `implicit` kwarg from the aten schema `aten::expand(Tensor(a) self, SymInt[] size, *, bool implicit=False)`.

Autograd emits `aten.expand.default(..., implicit=False)` internally — for example, from `mean_backward` and broadcast-backward paths. When dynamo captures such a graph and hands it to Inductor, the `register_lowering` wrapper forwards kwargs unchanged to the lowering, and `expand()` raises `TypeError: expand() got an unexpected keyword argument 'implicit'`. Every backward compile that flows through an autograd-generated expand fails with this error.

`implicit` is autograd-internal metadata (per aten's docs, "an internal flag used by PyTorch's autograd machinery") that does not affect the produced tensor. This diff makes the lowering accept and discard the kwarg. No output semantics change.

Test Plan:
Added a regression test `test_expand_implicit_kwarg` in `caffe2/test/inductor/test_torchinductor.py` that calls `torch.ops.aten.expand.default(x, sizes, implicit=False)` inside a compiled fn — mirrors what autograd emits.

```
$ buck2 test mode/opt //caffe2/test/inductor:test_inductor -- test_expand_implicit_kwarg
Tests finished: Pass 1. Fail 0.
```

Also verified end-to-end with an out-of-tree MTIA backward-compile harness — a suite of 7 minimal backward tests (`mean(a).backward()`, `mean(a * b).backward()`, `mean(a * b.unsqueeze(-1)).backward()`, `mean(a * b.expand(...)).backward()`, etc.) all failed pre-fix with the `implicit` `TypeError` and all pass post-fix.

Authored with AI assistant (Claude Code).

Differential Revision: D110394927']

['cudagraph_trees: honor mark_static_address outputs

cudagraph_trees classifies each storage that is live at the end of
capture as either a private-pool allocation or a known static
input. An output whose storage was flagged via
torch._dynamo.mark_static_address was neither, so check_memory_pool
reported it as "not allocated in pool but should be", and (with the
slow-path asserts off) the pool manager poisoned/freed it across
steps ("accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been
overwritten by a subsequent run").

mark_static_address marks a tensor's address as
constant/externally- managed; this honors that on outputs too. An
output carrying _dynamo_static_input_type (renamed to just
_dynamo_static_type) is now treated as a static output: excluded
from the warmup live-storage set (add_ref) and classified as a
static output at record time (_add_first_outputs), so it skips the
pool-membership check and per-step deallocation -- exactly as an
output aliasing a persistent static input already does.

Authored with Claude.']

['Add Python c10d Backend trampoline

Add PyBackend, a pybind trampoline for c10d::Backend, and bind the Backend APIs needed for Python subclasses to implement Backend-level collectives, p2p operations, fault-tolerance hooks, coalescing, metadata, tensor allocation, and lifecycle methods. Custom backend registration now owns the Python-object lifetime handoff inside ProcessGroup._register_backend, so there is no public _wrap_py_backend helper and native C++ Backend subclasses continue to register directly.

PyBackend lives in its own header, separate from the existing PyProcessGroup trampoline. ['[MPS] Enable ConvTranspose3d for fp16/bf16 via internal fp32 upcast

ConvTranspose3d (5-D transposed convolution) on the MPS backend hard-rejects
fp16 and bf16 inputs with "ConvTranspose 3D with BF16 or FP16 types is not
supported on MPS" (gh-160739). That gate was added in #154696, which enabled
the fp32/complex64 3D path but left half-precision types unsupported because
the CPU-vs-MPS accuracy gap in half precision was "too large to conclude
whether there is a bug ... without a more rigorous study."

Root cause: the op was never actually broken for half types, it was just
never attempted in fp32 in the first place, so nobody could tell whether a
prior fp16/bf16 accuracy complaint was a real kernel bug or ordinary
half-precision rounding. This commit sidesteps the question by never
computing in half precision at all: input/weight (and, in the backward
pass, grad_output) are upcast to fp32 before calling the existing,
already-validated fp32 forward/backward helpers, then the result is cast
back to the caller's original dtype. This also transitively fixes gh-160332
(torch.amp.autocast on MPS crashing on any module containing ConvTranspose3d,
since autocast always tries fp16/bf16 first).

While enabling the previously-xfailed/skipped fp16/bf16 test cases in
test_mps.py, the OpInfo consistency test intermittently failed on ~0.1-0.3%
of output elements. Investigated before assuming the upcast path was
correct: test_mps.py's _run_op already special-cases grid_sampler_2d/3d to
compare MPS output against a CPU fp32 reference (not CPU-native-fp16)
"since MPS uses float32 intermediates for these ops," but conv_transpose3d
was never added to that list despite this change making it true here too.
Confirmed empirically that this was a reference-quality artifact, not a
patch bug: for the failing sample, MPS-fp16 (this patch) has lower error
against a true fp32 reference (max abs err 1.07e-2) than CPU-native-fp16
does against that same reference (max abs err 4.03e-2) -- i.e. the patched
output is more accurate than the thing it was being compared against.
Extended the same fp32-reference exemption to conv_transpose3d.

Test Plan:

Built from source with USE_MPS=1 on Apple M5 Max / macOS 26.4.1:

    export USE_MPS=1
    export MACOSX_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET=13.0
    python setup.py develop

Confirmed the bug is fixed and matches a CPU fp32 reference within expected
fp16/bf16 rounding tolerance (mean abs error ~1e-4 for fp16, ~8e-4 for
bf16, vs ~3e-8 for the fp32 control):

    python test_convtranspose3d_repro.py
    python validate_convtranspose3d_patch.py --grad

Ran the upstream test suite covering this op, all passing except the
pre-existing (unrelated) int64 xfail:

    python test/test_mps.py -v -k "conv_transpose3d"
    python test/nn/test_convolution.py -v -k "ConvTranspose3d"

Also ran the broader conv test surface to check for regressions
(test/test_mps.py -k conv: 97 tests, 0 unexpected failures;
test/nn/test_convolution.py TestConvolutionNNDeviceTypeMPS: 526 tests, 2
pre-existing unrelated failures in test_conv3d_64bit_indexing_mps /
test_depthwise_conv_64bit_indexing_mps, which exercise _mps_convolution
rather than _mps_convolution_transpose and are unaffected by this change).

This PR was authored with the assistance of Claude (Anthropic).']

['[ROCm] Make amdsmi discovery robust to TheRock ROCm wheels layout

On TheRock ROCm wheels, `import torch` aborts with
`KeyError: 'libamd_smi.so'`: the amdsmi python package installs the native
library as a versioned soname under `_rocm_sdk_core/lib` (e.g.
`libamd_smi.so.26`), which amdsmi's own `find_smi_library()` fails to locate
when `$ROCM_HOME`/`$ROCM_PATH` and the loader path are unset. Its wrapper then
does `_libraries['libamd_smi.so']`, raising `KeyError`, which escapes the
existing guard (only `ModuleNotFoundError` was tolerated) and kills
`import torch`.

Two-part torch-side hardening (mirrors the `_preload_rocm_deps()` philosophy):

1. Make `_amdsmi_cdll_hook` TheRock-aware: locate `_rocm_sdk_core` via
   `importlib.util.find_spec` and add its `lib/libamd_smi.so*` (glob, incl.
   versioned sonames) as last-resort candidate paths; match names whose
   basename starts with `libamd_smi.so` so amdsmi's bare
   `CDLL("libamd_smi.so")` is redirected to the real versioned file. Existing
   `ROCM_HOME`/`ROCM_PATH` behavior is preserved; guarded to Linux.

2. Make a non-discoverable amdsmi non-fatal: a half-installed amdsmi (python
   package present, `.so` undiscoverable) now degrades to `_HAS_AMDSMI=False`
   instead of aborting `import torch`, matching the existing missing-module
   path.

Downstream follow-up to `_preload_rocm_deps()` (#188454) for the TheRock
wheels world.

Made with Cursor']

['[pytorch][triton_op] Honor caller-provided schema in triton_op

Summary:
torch.library.triton_op exposes and documents a `schema` parameter but ignores it: the body unconditionally computes `infer_schema(fn, mutates_args=mutates_args)` and passes that to custom_op. This makes it impossible to register a triton_op whose signature infer_schema cannot express, e.g. an optional-tensor return `(Tensor, Tensor?)`.

Gate on the caller-provided `schema` when present, falling back to `infer_schema(...)` otherwise, matching what torch.library.custom_op already does. When `schema is None` (the default) the behavior is byte-identical, so this is a pure widening. The fbcode and xplat copies of caffe2/torch/_library/triton.py are updated in lockstep.

Differential Revision: D110283291']

OSDC mount. Compile in place on B200's writable host mount; on OSDC use a

['torch/fx: fix _format_target to handle Python keywords and embedded quotes

_format_target generates Python source code for module attribute accesses
in FX graphs. It previously used only str.isidentifier() to decide
between dot notation (self.foo) and getattr notation (getattr(self, "foo")).
This had two bugs:

1. Python keywords (e.g. "class", "return") pass isidentifier() but are
   invalid as attribute names in dot notation: self.seq.class is a
   SyntaxError.

2. Names containing double quotes were interpolated into a double-quoted
   string literal (f'getattr({r}, "{e}")'), producing malformed code like
   getattr(self, "key"name").

The fix adds keyword.iskeyword(e) to the condition (the keyword module is
already imported in graph.py) and switches to {e!r} so Python's repr()
handles all quoting and escaping correctly.

Fixes #188538.

Authored with the assistance of an AI assistant.

Test Plan:
Running the new test locally requires a built PyTorch. The logic was
verified in isolation:

  python -c "
  import keyword
  def _format_target(base, target):
      elems = target.split('.')
      r = base
      for e in elems:
          if not e.isidentifier() or keyword.iskeyword(e):
              r = f'getattr({r}, {e!r})'
          else:
              r = f'{r}.{e}'
      return r
  print(_format_target('self', 'seq.class'))   # getattr(self.seq, 'class')
  print(_format_target('self', 'key\"name'))   # getattr(self, 'key\"name')
  print(_format_target('self', 'linear'))      # self.linear
  "

A new test test_format_target_special_names in test/test_fx.py covers
both the keyword case (submodule named 'class') and the embedded-quote
case (submodule named 'key"name'), verifying that symbolic_trace produces
valid Python and the traced module returns the correct output.']

['[Profiler] Exclude Python function events from key_averages() by default

PR #178168 removed the C++ filter that previously excluded Python events
from kinetoEvents to achieve events()/Chrome Trace parity. The unintended
side effect is that key_averages() now includes Python-internal events like
threading.py(359): wait as top hotspots, obscuring real operator-level
breakdown (fixes #188520).

Adds include_python_functions=False to key_averages() on all three public
entry points (EventList, profile, _KinetoProfile). Python frame events are
skipped by default; pass include_python_functions=True to restore the full
per-callsite view.

Test Plan:

```
python test/profiler/test_profiler.py \
  TestProfiler.test_key_averages_excludes_python_functions_by_default
```

Co-authored with an AI assistant (Claude).']

['[c10d] Deprecate setSequenceNumberForGroup as a no-op

Sequence numbers always start at 0 on every rank now, so there is no
initial value for ranks to agree on, and setSequenceNumberForGroup() has
been an empty no-op for every backend (NCCL/Gloo/UCC) for a long time.
This removes the dead machinery while keeping the Backend-level binding
so existing downstream callers keep working.

Backend::setSequenceNumberForGroup() now emits a one-time deprecation
warning and does nothing. The _set_sequence_number_for_group binding is
kept on Backend because downstream projects call it on a backend object
(e.g. vLLM calls backend_class._set_sequence_number_for_group() on a
ProcessGroupGloo). The ProcessGroup method and its binding, the redundant
per-backend overrides, the ProcessGroupWrapper's internal call, and the
internal callers in init_process_group/split_group are removed. The
_get_sequence_number_for_group path used for desync detection is
unchanged.

Test Plan:

Build:

```
pip install -e . -v --no-build-isolation
```

Behavior check (Backend keeps a warning no-op, ProcessGroup binding
removed, getter intact, no exception):

```
import os, warnings, torch, torch.distributed as dist
os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost"
os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "29577"
dist.init_process_group("gloo", rank=0, world_size=1)
pg = dist.group.WORLD
b = pg._get_backend(torch.device("cpu"))
assert not hasattr(pg, "_set_sequence_number_for_group")
assert hasattr(b, "_set_sequence_number_for_group")
b._set_sequence_number_for_group()  # warns once, no-op, does not raise
assert b._get_sequence_number_for_group() == 0
dist.destroy_process_group()
```

Lint:

```
lintrunner -a
```']

['[inductor] Fix CUDA "misaligned address" regression from autotune module unload (#184285)

Summary:
#184285 began unloading static-launcher CUDA/HIP modules after autotuning. On CUDA this crashed APS workflows with `CUDA error: misaligned address` (T270390770).

Cause: the cached `_FastCudaLauncher` bakes a raw `CUfunction` and `_build_fast_launcher` replaces the `runner` global, dropping the launcher's only reference to the owning kernel. The kernel is then garbage-collected, its `__del__` unloads the module, and the baked pointer dangles.

Fix: `_build_fast_launcher` keeps a `_static_kernel_owner` reference so the module stays loaded while the launcher is callable. Unload still happens once the launcher is dropped, preserving the ROCm module-table fix on both backends.

Test Plan:
New CPU-only unit test `test_fast_launcher_keeps_kernel_owner_alive` in `test_static_triton_launcher.py`: builds a fast launcher (faked `_FastCudaLauncher`), drops all other references, and asserts the owning kernel survives `gc.collect()` only via `_static_kernel_owner`, then is released once the launcher is dropped.

```
buck2 test //caffe2/test/inductor:static_triton_launcher
```

On a CUDA host, confirm an exhaustive-autotuning APS-style compile no longer raises `CUDA error: misaligned address`. On ROCm, confirm long exhaustive tuning still releases modules (no `hipErrorNoBinaryForGpu`).

Reviewed By: izaitsevfb, jansel

Differential Revision: D110227033




Signed-off-by: Jean Schmidt <jschmidt@meta.com>']

Introduces skipIfRocmVersionAtLeast([7, 14]) and its multiprocess
counterpart skip_if_rocm_ver_atleast_multiprocess([7, 14]), then applies
them to the tests that fail on ROCm 7.14 but pass on 7.2.x. These skips
are dormant on the current 7.2 CI and activate automatically when the
rocm-preview lane switches to the TheRock 7.14 wheel, keeping the suite

['[INITIAL] This is a new test file being added, so I'll write the commit message describing it.

Add multi-rank GPU sanity test for ProcessGroupNCCLTC

Add test/distributed/test_c10d_nccltc.py, a multi-process GPU sanity
suite for the in-tree torchcomms NCCL backend
(c10d::nccltc::ProcessGroupNCCLTC), selected via
init_process_group(backend="nccltc"). Modeled on the torchcomms c10d
tests, it runs against real NCCL on 2+ GPUs using
MultiProcContinuousTest.

The suite covers the core collectives and point-to-point paths:
all_reduce, broadcast, all_gather, all_gather_into_tensor,
reduce_scatter_tensor, all_to_all_single, barrier, send/recv, and
batch_isend_irecv. Each test asserts the numerically expected result so
the backend's data movement, not just its API surface, is exercised.

The backend is normally discovered through the
torch.distributed.backends entry point. The test also registers it
explicitly via _register_builtin_nccltc_backend() so it stays
self-contained under editable installs, where a stale repo egg-info can
shadow the dist-info entry points; the call short-circuits if the
backend is already registered. Tests require NCCL and at least 2 GPUs,
and only run when CUDA is available.

turns the backend on end to end, so that

`c10d::nccltc::ProcessGroupNCCLTC` over real NCCL.

  binding (`ensureInitialized`), and the c10d virtual overrides that
  translate c10d option/tensor shapes to the internal engine and return
  `c10d::Work` (the role `BackendWrapper` played upstream): broadcast,
  allreduce(+coalesced), reduce, allgather (with an aliased-output slow path
  via a contiguous staging tensor), `_allgather_base`, gather, scatter,
  reduce_scatter(+tensor_coalesced), `_reduce_scatter_base`, alltoall /

  scaling factor from `NCCLPreMulSumSupplement`); `operationTimeout` maps
  c10d's `kUnsetTimeout` (-1) to the comm default. Empty/coalescing-window
  works return a small `CompletedWorkTC` sentinel.

['[INITIAL] [c10d][nccltc] Add the ProcessGroupNCCLTC NCCL engine implementation

Fifth commit of the ProcessGroupNCCLTC stack. Adds the new source file
`torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/nccltc/ProcessGroupNCCLTC.cpp`, which provides the
NCCL engine backing the ProcessGroupNCCLTC backend, ported from
`TorchCommNCCL.cpp`.

The file implements:
- Lifecycle: `init()` (bootstraps the communicator through the Store-based
  `TorchCommNCCLBootstrap`, sets up the internal stream, dependency event,
  barrier buffer, event pool, and timeout watchdog thread), `finalize()`,
  `abort()`, `revokeNcclComm()`/`abortNcclComm()`, and a destructor that warns
  on un-finalized teardown and best-effort aborts the communicator.
- The collective and point-to-point engine: `broadcastImpl`, `all_reduce`,
  `reduceImpl`, `all_gather(_v)`, `all_gather_single`,
  `reduce_scatter(_v)(_single)`, `all_to_all`, `all_to_all(_v)_single`,
  `scatterImpl`, `gatherImpl`, `barrierImpl`, `sendImpl`, `recvImpl`, and
  `batch_op_issue`, each wrapping the work-tracking/event-recording flow.
- `NCCLException` and small helpers (`getCommPtr`, `getOperationTimeout`).

Notable details ported/adapted here:
- `all_to_all_single` uses the native `ncclAllToAll` on NCCL >= 2.28 and falls
  back to grouped send/recv on older versions.
- Reconfigurable mode never aborts the process: `abort()`/`abortNcclComm()` route
  to `revokeNcclComm()` (idempotent via an exchange on `revoked_`, safe to call
  from both the watchdog thread and a synchronous collective) so failures can be
  handled by reconfiguring.
- The destructor detaches (rather than joins) the timeout thread when invoked
  from within that thread to avoid a self-join deadlock.

Source-only; wired into the build in the activation commit.

Differences from upstream torchcomms:
- Method names that collide with `c10d::Backend` virtuals are suffixed `Impl`
  (broadcastImpl, reduceImpl, scatterImpl, gatherImpl, barrierImpl, sendImpl,
  recvImpl); bodies are otherwise close to verbatim.
- The bootstrap is given rank/size explicitly; eager `init()` is driven lazily
  by the c10d surface in the activation commit.
- The `TorchCommFactory` "nccl" backend + allocator registration block and the
  glog-init call are removed; registration moves to the c10d entry points and
  the allocator to `getMemAllocator`.
- namespace/include-path rewrite.

["[INITIAL] [c10d][nccltc] Port the NcclApi abstraction

Second commit of the ProcessGroupNCCLTC stack. Adds the `NcclApi` interface
and its `DefaultNcclApi` implementation under
`torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/nccltc/`, the thin wrapper layer over the NCCL C
API that the backend uses for collectives (allreduce/broadcast/allgather/
reducescatter/alltoall), point-to-point send/recv, communicator lifecycle
(init/abort/destroy/revoke/split/shrink/grow), group start/end, reduction-op
create/destroy, mem alloc/free, memory registration, and the window/one-sided
RMA operations (window register/deregister, put/signal/wait-signal). Keeping
it as an injectable virtual interface preserves upstream's testability via
dependency injection.

Each `DefaultNcclApi` method takes a mutex and forwards to the underlying
NCCL call. Calls that depend on newer NCCL are version-guarded by
`NCCL_VERSION_CODE`: the window/RMA, shrink, grow, revoke, alltoall, and
comm-get-unique-id wrappers return `ncclInvalidUsage` (and log) on older NCCL,
while register/dereg and mem alloc/free throw. The header also provides
fallback definitions of `NCCL_SHRINK_ABORT`, an opaque `ncclWindow_t` alias,
and `NCCL_WIN_COLL_SYMMETRIC` so the interface declares unconditionally
against older NCCL headers; the version guards keep those values from being
read at runtime.

["[dynamo] Add polyfills for operator.contains and operator.call
operator.contains(a, b) is equivalent to b in a (note reversed operands).
operator.call(obj, /, *args, **kwargs) is equivalent to obj(*args, **kwargs)
and was added in Python 3.11.
Without these polyfills, using either function inside a torch.compile region
causes a graph break with 'Failed to trace builtin operator'.
Fixes part of #116396.

['[PT2 Export] Fix `SymNumberMemoDescriptor` to support inference FakeTensors (#188575)

Summary:

`SymNumberMemoDescriptor.__set__` previously skipped memo storage for inference
FakeTensors (guarded by `elif not obj.is_inference()`). This caused every
`nonzero()` call on inference-mode boolean mask tensors to allocate a fresh
unbacked symint, even when the same mask was reused across multiple boolean
indexing operations.

This led to `GuardOnDataDependentSymNode` errors during AOTINDUCTOR lowering
when `torch.export` tried to verify that two tensors filtered by the same
boolean mask had equal sizes -- the tracer saw independent symbols (e.g.,
`Eq(u2322 + 1, u2323 + 1)`) instead of the same unified symbol.

The fix:
- `__set__`: Always store the memo value. Skip only the `_version` storage
  for inference tensors (since they do not track version counters).
- `__get__`: Skip the version counter staleness check for inference tensors.
  Only validate the epoch. Use `getattr(..., None)` defaults for safety.

Test Plan:
UT:
```
buck2 test fbcode//mode/dev-nosan fbcode//caffe2/test:fake_tensor -- -- -k test_inference_mode_nonzero_memo 
```

Without the fix, this UT fails at `self.assertTrue(statically_known_true(sx == sy))`

E2E:

Built ephemeral `ien.lower.cpu` package with this fix
- Ran RE client lowering repro for model 1060790975 snapshot 31
- Verified `GuardOnDataDependentSymNode` error is eliminated
- Export completes successfully, lowering proceeds to AOTI compilation
- Local `torch.export` tests with boolean indexing continue to pass
- Debug paste: https://fburl.com/everpaste/8gzt64ng

Differential Revision: D106102842']

['Preserve pipeline buffers during dynamic metadata inference

PipelineStage dynamic metadata inference runs representative forwards and backwards before the first runtime microbatch. That is needed to infer runtime metadata, but the eager executions can mutate module buffers through forward code or backward hooks. Those mutations leak into the real training step even though metadata inference is not part of training.

Save and restore the stage module buffers around dynamic forward and backward metadata inference so those eager probes can observe the same buffer values without publishing their mutations. The restore is scoped to dynamic inference only; static metadata still avoids the eager probes entirely.

Add a regression test with a module that mutates a buffer in both forward and a backward hook, then verifies PipelineStage preparation leaves the buffer unchanged.

Test Plan:

```bash

conda run -n pt-dev lintrunner -a
```

['[release/2.13] Bump binary build timeout 280 -> 400 minutes

The manywheel CUDA 12.9 build (manywheel-cuda-cu129-build) on the
v2.13.0 release candidates is being cancelled by the 280-minute job
timeout. It is not hung and not a build error: the build progresses
steadily and was at "[707/712] Install the project" on the final wheel
when killed, i.e. essentially complete after ~285 minutes.

Root cause: the first wheel does a full from-scratch CUDA compile of the
whole arch list (7.5;8.0;8.6;9.0;10.0;12.0+PTX), which now includes the
Blackwell sm_100/sm_120 arches added for 12.9. That first build takes
~3h (flash_attention CUDA TUs dominate); the remaining ~5 Python wheels
reuse the objects at ~18 min each, totaling just over the 280-minute cap.
Release binary builds do not use sccache, so the first build is always
from scratch.

Rather than trim the arch list (Blackwell support is intended for 12.9),
give the builds 2 more hours of headroom by raising the shared
common.timeout_minutes from 280 to 400. This also bumps the other Linux
manywheel builds (cpu/cu126/cu130/cu132) and the macOS-arm64 wheel build,
which share the same value; the change only raises a ceiling and has no
effect on builds that finish sooner. Windows binary builds use a separate
value (timeout_minutes_windows_binary=360) and are unchanged.

Test Plan:
Regenerated workflows and confirmed the diff is limited to the timeout bump:

```
python3 .github/scripts/generate_ci_workflows.py
git diff .github/workflows/  # only "timeout-minutes: 280" -> "400"
```

Authored with assistance from Claude.']

["[Inductor] Fix log_sigmoid_forward flushing float32 subnormals to +0.0 on CUDA

Triton's CUDA backend runs with FTZ enabled by default (disable_ftz: False
in triton_meta).  NVIDIA's libdevice.log1pf is not called as an opaque
intrinsic; it is inlined by LLVM as a polynomial approximation using
fma.rn.ftz.f32 instructions.  For subnormal inputs, the final
reconstructing FMA in that polynomial is itself FTZ-tagged and flushes the
correctly-computed-but-subnormal result to zero.  This is distinct from
exp()'s output being flushed -- exp() survives correctly (confirmed via
isolated PTX dump of the libdevice kernel); the loss happens specifically
inside log1p's own codegen, on its output.

For float32 x >= ~87.3, logsigmoid(x) ~= -exp(-x) underflows into the
subnormal range (~6e-39 for x=88), hitting this path and corrupting
torch.signbit, y == 0, and torch.copysign comparisons.

Fix: exclude aten.log_sigmoid_forward from Inductor's decomposition table
and register a device-conditional lowering.  CUDA delegates to the native
ATen kernel (compiled by nvcc without -ftz); CPU and other backends keep
the inline decomposition to preserve fusion, since they have no FTZ issue.

Buffer semantics verified from Activation.cpp:799 -- the CUDA backward
kernel ignores the buffer entirely (only the CPU backward reads it), so
routing CUDA through FallbackKernel produces the correct empty buffer and
there is no backward regression.

Verified on RTX 4070 Laptop (SM 89), CUDA 12.6: forward values, signbit,
and backward gradients (including subnormal gradients at x=88/90/100) all
match exactly between eager and compiled after the fix.

Test plan:
```
python test/inductor/test_cuda_repro.py -k test_logsigmoid_subnormal_outputs -v
python test/inductor/test_cuda_repro.py -k test_logsigmoid_subnormal_backward -v
```

Fixes #188541

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>", 'Narrow CUDA fallback to float32 only (address review feedback)

FTZ applies only to float32 PTX instructions in CUDA.  float64 subnormals
are preserved regardless of the FTZ kernel flag, and float16 logsigmoid
outputs at large x already underflow to zero in float16 precision (min
float16 subnormal ~6e-8 >> exp(-88) ~6e-39).

Gate the FallbackKernel path on dtype == float32 so float16, bfloat16, and
float64 continue to use the fused inline decomposition on CUDA.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>']

['[sparse] Add size validation in _convert_indices_from_coo_to_csr

Fixes #182064

When `_convert_indices_from_coo_to_csr` is called with a negative size parameter,
it previously allocated a zero-element output tensor (size + 1 = 0) and then
attempted to write beyond its bounds, causing illegal memory access on both CPU
and CUDA.

This adds an O(1) host-side check `TORCH_CHECK(size >= 0, ...)` in the meta
function, placed next to the existing dimension validation. This converts the
undefined behavior (illegal CUDA write / CPU heap overflow) into a clean
RuntimeError with a clear error message.

The fix follows PyTorch's design philosophy for internal conversion ops:
- O(1) host-side checks are acceptable (no measurable cost)
- Checks requiring device reduction/sync are not added
- The op remains "trust the caller" for the `max(self) <= size` invariant

All internal callers already pass valid size values (e.g., `self.size(0)`),
so this issue only affects direct calls with invalid inputs (e.g., fuzzing).

Test Plan:
- Added test case for negative size in test_sparse_csr.py
- Verified original crash case now raises RuntimeError
- Existing tests pass']

['[test stats] Sanitize empty-file pytest rows at the upload boundary

pytest's xunit2 schema drops the file/line testcase attrs and emits a
dotted, package-path classname (e.g. "test.test_ops.TestCommonCPU").
torch/testing/_internal/common_utils.py::sanitize_pytest_xml normally
re-derives `file` from that classname in-process right after pytest runs.
But pytest.main() and that sanitize pass are sequential statements in the
same subprocess, so if the subprocess is killed between them (shard
timeout, OOM, teardown crash) the XML is uploaded unsanitized: empty
`file` + dotted classname. Those rows leak to tests.all_test_runs and
break consumers that key on `file` (e.g. the auto-revert signal
extractor, which collapses the empty file into a bogus tests-to-include
target).

Re-apply the same transformation at parse_xml_report -- the single upload
chokepoint that both the streaming (tools/testing/upload_artifacts.py)
and post-job (backfill_test_jsons_while_running) paths route through. The
helper is idempotent and conservative: it only rewrites the broken shape
(empty `file` + dotted classname) and leaves rows that already have a
`file` untouched, so healthy pytest/unittest rows are never modified.

Test Plan: python tools/test/test_upload_test_stats.py']

['[inductor] Fix OrderingBarrier dependency keys in control_deps lowering

Two bugs were introduced in commit 5f7558b34e1 ("[AO] Use OrderingBarrier
for control_deps pass-through ordering"):

**Bug 1 -- wrong keys/values in additional_buffer_deps for passthrough barriers
(torch/_inductor/lowering.py)**

`control_deps_op_lowering` registers passthrough OrderingBarrier nodes in
`V.graph.additional_buffer_deps` so that `compute_dependencies` can later
enforce ordering.  The dict is keyed by *operation name* (e.g. "op_K",
returned by `node.get_name()` on a BaseSchedulerNode) and its values are
*buffer names* (e.g. "buf_R", used as keys in `name_to_users`).

The buggy code wrote:
  additional_buffer_deps[barrier.get_name()].add(op_name)
                         ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^  ^^^^^^^
  barrier.get_name() returns the BUFFER name "buf_X" -- wrong key.
  op_name = op.operation_name is the OPERATION name "op_M" -- wrong value.

Both are backwards.  The fix:
  additional_buffer_deps[barrier.get_operation_name()].add(op.get_name())

`barrier.get_operation_name()` is the operation name (correct key);
`op.get_name()` on an ExternKernel Buffer IR node returns the buffer name
(correct value).

**Bug 2 -- `ordering_only` attribute read from SchedulerBuffer instead of
its wrapped IR node (torch/_inductor/scheduler.py)**

In `compute_dependencies`, the mutation loop reads:
  is_ordering_only = getattr(buf, "ordering_only", False)

`buf` is a `SchedulerBuffer` dataclass which has no `ordering_only`
attribute, so this always evaluates to False.  The attribute lives on the
underlying IR node (`buf.node`), which for `OrderingBarrier` is set to
True as a class attribute.

The fix:
  is_ordering_only = getattr(buf.node, "ordering_only", False)

When True, the code takes the weak-dependency path (add WeakDep on the
renamed alias, skip the strong StarDep + for-user WeakDep loop).  Together
with Bug 1's fix, this ensures that `barrier.get_operation_name()` is
looked up correctly in `additional_buffer_deps` and the barrier is ordered
after all subgraph ops via a clean WeakDep chain rather than relying on the
accidental side-effect of the strong-mutation for-user loop.

These bugs cause incorrect tensor values in bidirectional CUDA stream
synchronization patterns compiled by `torch.compile`, as seen in the
disabled test `test_bidirectional_stream_sync` (issue #188513, ~18.8% of
elements wrong, max diff ~12.5).

Test Plan:

The fix re-enables test_bidirectional_stream_sync in
test/inductor/test_user_streams.py.  That test requires a CUDA GPU and
cannot be run in this environment (Intel Iris Xe only), so the fix is
validated by code-level reasoning:

1. `barrier.get_operation_name()` matches the key format read by
   `compute_dependencies` (`node.get_name()` on BaseSchedulerNode returns
   `self.node.get_operation_name()`).

2. `op.get_name()` on ExternKernel IR nodes returns the buffer name, which
   is what `add_user` and `WeakDep` expect as their dep argument.

3. `buf.node.ordering_only` correctly reaches the class-level `True` on
   `OrderingBarrier`, enabling the weak-dep path that was designed for it.

The existing tests in test/inductor/test_control_deps.py cover the
IR-level OrderingBarrier properties and continue to pass.']

['Fix OrderingBarrier additional_buffer_deps key mismatch in control_deps lowering

The OrderingBarrier pass-through versioning in control_deps_op_lowering was
storing additional_buffer_deps under the barrier's buffer name (via
barrier.get_name(), which returns the Buffer name, e.g. "buf5"). However, the
scheduler looks up additional_buffer_deps using node.get_name(), which for
NopKernelSchedulerNode returns the operation name (via get_operation_name(),
e.g. "op2"). Since these names differ, the scheduler never saw the dependency
between the OrderingBarrier and its subgraph ops (record_event/wait_event).

Without these deps, the barrier had no ordering constraint relative to the
sync ops it was supposed to be ordered after. In bidirectional stream sync
patterns (stream A records event, stream B waits and records back, stream A
waits again), this allowed the scheduler to place compute kernels before the
necessary wait_event calls, causing GPU reads of data not yet written by the
other stream -- a classic stream synchronization race condition that manifests
as flaky numerical mismatches.

The fix uses barrier.get_operation_name() to store the deps under the correct
key that matches what the scheduler looks up.

Test Plan:

The fix was verified by analyzing the mismatch between Buffer.get_name() and
Operation.get_operation_name() in the OperationBuffer hierarchy. The
OrderingBarrier (NopKernel -> InputsKernel -> OperationBuffer) has distinct
buffer and operation names assigned by register_buffer() and
register_operation() respectively. The scheduler's _init_dependencies lookups
at line 4755 use the operation name, confirming the key mismatch.

```
python -m pytest test/inductor/test_user_streams.py::TestUserStreamCompile::test_bidirectional_stream_sync -xvs
```

Authored with an AI assistant.', "Fix OrderingBarrier additional_buffer_deps to use buffer names instead of operation names

The control_deps_op_lowering function stores dependencies in
V.graph.additional_buffer_deps for the scheduler to enforce ordering.
The scheduler looks up these dependency names in self.name_to_buf,
which maps buffer names (e.g. 'buf5') to SchedulerBuffer objects.

A prior fix correctly changed the KEY from buffer name to operation name
so the scheduler could find the deps. However, the VALUES were still
operation names (e.g. 'op1'), which don't exist in name_to_buf. This
caused KeyError in Scheduler.compute_ancestors() when it did
self.name_to_buf[dep.name] on those operation-name deps.

The fix changes the VALUES from op.operation_name to op.get_name()
(the buffer name), matching the convention used by the other
additional_buffer_deps site in the same function (line 9001) where
dep_name comes from dep_ir_node.get_name(). The isinstance(op, ir.Buffer)
guard follows the same pattern used by void_names earlier in the function.

Test Plan:
```
python -m pytest test/inductor/test_torchinductor.py -k test_copy_non_blocking_is_pinned -xvs
python -m pytest test/inductor/test_user_streams.py -k test_bidirectional_stream_sync -xvs

['Fix URL lint false-FAIL on bot-protected hosts (hud.pytorch.org)

The check-host.net fallback in lint_urls.sh already probes URLs from an
external vantage point when the runner cannot reach the host directly,
but its result-acceptance condition only adopted 2xx/3xx statuses. WAF
responses (403/429/503) that we treat as WARN downstream were discarded,
leaving code=000 and producing a false FAIL for live-but-bot-protected
hosts such as hud.pytorch.org.

Adopt any real HTTP status (100-599) reported by check-host.net and let
the existing WARN/FAIL/OK logic classify it.

Fixes #188510']

when parameters come from different DeviceMesh objects. The subsequent

outputs silently. This adds a ValueError at construction time to catch this
invalid parameter combination.']

['Allow pre-split pipeline microbatches

Pipeline schedules currently assume step() inputs are full batches: the first
thing both single- and multi-stage schedules do is split args, kwargs, and
target into microbatches. That makes it impossible for input pipelines that
already produce microbatches to feed them directly without being split a second
time.

Add pre_split_args_kwargs=False to the schedule step path. With the flag
enabled, step() consumes the existing schedule-internal microbatch shape:
arg_mbs as the sole positional argument, kwarg_mbs via kwargs=..., and target
as target_mbs. The default stays false so existing callers continue through
automatic splitting. The implementation centralizes the mode switch before
schedule execution so all schedule subclasses share the same behavior and
validation.

```

["[inductor] cpp_wrapper: box None as undefined tensor for non-optional Tensor args (#188485)

Summary:

When cpp_wrapper boxed-dispatches a custom op (the path that SymInt-bearing ops take), a `None` at a non-optional `Tensor` argument was boxed as `c10::IValue()` (None), which fails to unbox as `const Tensor&` with `RuntimeError: Expected Tensor but got None`. This hit fbgemm TBE ops such as `split_embedding_codegen_forward_unweighted_pt2_wrapper`, whose `indice_weights` is `None` in the unweighted case: eager materializes it to an undefined tensor via `value_or(Tensor())`, but that C++ materialization is absent from the compiled graph.

Fix: in `codegen_ivalue`, box a `None` at a non-optional `Tensor` arg as an undefined `at::Tensor()` (ATen's canonical absent-tensor sentinel), matching eager. Genuine `Tensor?` optionals still box as `None`.

Authored with AI assistance.

Reviewed By: kqfu

Differential Revision: D109651557"]

['[MPS] Add complex64 support to linalg.lu_factor

torch.linalg.lu_factor and the ops that decompose through it (det, slogdet, lu)
raised "MPS doesn't support complex types" on the MPS backend. The float path
wraps Apple's MPSMatrixDecompositionLU, which is float-only, so complex had no
implementation.

Add a Metal kernel implementing LU factorization with partial pivoting (LAPACK
getf2 semantics) for complex64, mirroring the existing svd_jacobi/eigh complex
kernels in LinearAlgebra.metal. The float32 path is unchanged. complex64
lu_factor, lu_factor_ex, det, slogdet, and lu now work on MPS.

Notes:
- Pivot ranking uses cabs1 = |re| + |im| (matching LAPACK icamax), so the pivots
  and the packed LU match the CPU reference element-for-element (Euclidean
  magnitude would break ties differently).
- Plain LU is conjugation-free (no inner products / adjoint), unlike the
  svd/eigh/cholesky kernels.
- det/slogdet hand lu_factor a column-major LU, so the kernel runs on a
  row-major scratch and the result is copied back into LU. Matrices too large
  for threadgroup memory fall back to CPU.
- complex128/float64 remain unsupported on MPS and fall back to CPU.
- det/slogdet backward for complex64 (needs complex solve/inv) is not
  implemented and is xfailed as a follow-up.

Test plan:

    python -m pytest test/test_mps.py -k "test_output_match and (linalg_lu_factor or linalg_det or linalg_slogdet or linalg_lu_mps)" -q
    python -m pytest test/test_mps.py -k "test_linalg_lu_factor_ex or test_linalg_lu_factor_singular or test_linalg_det" -q
    python -m pytest test/test_ops.py -k "mps and (linalg_det or linalg_slogdet or linalg_lu_factor or linalg_lu_mps)" -q
    python -m pytest test/test_mps.py -k "linalg" -q

Forward results match CPU/LAPACK (reconstruction error ~1e-7); the broad linalg
sweep introduces no new failures.

Fixes #187107', 'Address review: rename kernel, parametrize tests, enable complex64 logdet

Renames lu_factor_complex64_mps to lu_factor_mps_kernel since the kernel also
handles float32, so complex64 in the name was misleading. Parametrizes
test_linalg_lu_factor_ex and test_linalg_det over [float32, complex64] via
@parametrize instead of an inner loop.

Adds logdet to SUPPORTED_COMPLEX_OPS: enabling complex64 lu_factor makes logdet
(logdet -> slogdet -> lu_factor) pass, which CI flagged as an unexpected success
against its stale expectedFailure. Its complex64 xfails are narrowed to
noncontiguous inputs and backward (test_variant_consistency_eager), matching
det/slogdet; forward complex64 logdet/det/slogdet now match CPU.

Test plan:

    python -m pytest test/test_ops.py -k "mps and (linalg_det or linalg_slogdet or linalg_lu_factor or linalg_lu_mps or logdet)" -q
    python -m pytest test/test_mps.py -k "test_linalg_lu_factor_ex or test_linalg_det or linalg" -q']

['[inductor] Bucket all_reduce in the manual overlap scheduler

The manual overlap bucketer (ManualOverlapPreservingBucketer) only handled
all_gather and reduce_scatter and silently skipped all_reduce. As a result the
HSDP replicate-axis / DDP gradient all_reduce was left unbucketed (one launch
per parameter) and never scheduled to overlap compute.

Teach the manual scheduler to treat all_reduce like reduce_scatter (both are
backward gradient collectives whose wait can be deferred past compute):
- bucket it via merge_all_reduce_bucket in _bucket_group
- accept it in manual_bucket_collectives
- defer its wait in _move_overlap_nodes and the base _manual_reorder_graph loop

merge_all_reduce_bucket and the all_reduce bucket key already exist in
bucketing.py and the auto bucketer already supports all_reduce; only the manual
path was missing it.

Add manual-bucketing tests covering all_reduce (no-bucket, separate buckets,
single bucket).', '[inductor] Split dependent collectives before manual bucketing

manual_bucket_collectives grouped same-key collectives (same group, reduce_op,
dtype) into one bucket without checking dependencies between them. Loss-parallel
cross-entropy emits a sumexp and a result sum-all_reduce on the same bucket key
where result depends on sumexp's output; fusing them makes the merged
collective's input depend on its own output -- a graph cycle that failed region
topological sort ("stable topological sort of region failed").

Partition each key-group into dependency-free sub-buckets before merging, so
only mutually independent collectives fuse. Each collective is placed in the
bucket equal to its Mirsky level (longest same-key dependency chain ending at
it), computed with a single topological forward pass over the scope's nodes
(O(scope) rather than O(N^2) pairwise ancestor queries). Same-level collectives
are independent, so this is the minimum number of cycle-free buckets.

Add a unit test reproducing the loss-parallel pattern.']

['Fix build with BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND=0

When BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND=0, the TS backend source files (including
ts_backend_impl.cpp defining InitTorchScriptBackend) are excluded from
libtorch at the CMake level. However, torch/csrc/lazy/python/init.cpp
still referenced these symbols unconditionally -- the existing guards
only checked FBCODE_CAFFE2/OVRSOURCE, not BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND. Since
BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND was never exposed as a C++ preprocessor define,
the code had no way to know the backend was absent, resulting in an
undefined symbol error at import time.

The fix adds BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND as a compile definition on the
torch_python CMake target (following the same pattern as USE_NUMPY and
USE_CUFILE), and gates all TS-backend-specific code in init.cpp on it.

Authored with the help of an AI assistant (Claude Code).

Test Plan:
Built with BUILD_LAZY_TS_BACKEND=0 and verified torch imports:
```
BUILD_CONFIG USE_CUDA=1 spin develop
python -c "import torch"  # previously failed with undefined symbol
```']

["fix(agents): allow directory names containing '..' in FilesystemFileSearchMiddleware

_validate_and_resolve_path used ''..' in path' which rejected any path
whose string representation contained '..', including legitimate directory
names like 'src..old' or 'v1..backup' (issue #38549).

Change the check to inspect individual path components via Path(path).parts,
blocking only components that are exactly '..'. The tilde check and the
_is_within_root containment guard remain in place as additional defenses.

Adds regression tests for:
- glob_search accepting a path like '/src..old'
- grep_search accepting a path like '/v1..backup'
- real traversal ('/../') still blocked after the fix", "fix(openrouter): add close/aclose and context manager support to prevent httpx leak

When app_url or app_title are set (both default to non-None values),
_build_client() creates new httpx.Client and httpx.AsyncClient instances
that are never closed, leaking TCP connections. Under high concurrency
this exhausts ephemeral ports and causes OSError (issue #38610).

Fix:
- Store the httpx clients as Pydantic fields (http_client, async_http_client)
  excluded from serialization so they don't affect serialization behaviour.
- Add close() to release the sync httpx.Client.
- Add aclose() to release both clients asynchronously.
- Add __enter__/__exit__ and __aenter__/__aexit__ for context-manager use.

Example usage:
    async with ChatOpenRouter(model='anthropic/claude-3-haiku') as llm:
        result = await llm.ainvoke('Hello')

6 new unit tests cover close(), aclose(), and both context managers."]

["fix(openai): preserve Pydantic schema for structured output on Responses API

When `reasoning` forces the Responses API path, `with_structured_output()`
converts the Pydantic class to a dict via `_convert_to_openai_response_format`,
losing the original class reference. This causes `_generate`/`_agenerate` to
call `responses.create()` instead of `responses.parse()`, so servers that don't
enforce structured output return plain text that fails Pydantic validation.

Fix: pass the original Pydantic class through a private `_pydantic_response_format`
kwarg so both `_construct_responses_api_payload` (sets `text_format`) and
`_generate`/`_agenerate` (chooses `responses.parse()`) can use it.

Also adds bidirectional `reasoning` dict → `reasoning_effort` translation on the
Chat Completions path, so `use_responses_api=False` works as an escape hatch
without raising `TypeError`.

Assisted-by: Claude Opus 4.6"]
